quizQuestion:
Lo hago {___} ti — quiero ayudarte.
(I do it for you — I want to help you. [on your behalf])
sentenceTranslation: I do it for you — I want to help you.
grammarPointLabel: por — on behalf of / for someone's sake
explanation: por — used when doing something on behalf of someone or for their sake. Contrast: 'para ti' = for you (recipient/gift), 'por ti' = for your sake/on your behalf.
por — 'gracias por' = thank you for. In 'gracias por todo', 'por' expresses the reason/cause for gratitude. Always 'gracias por', never 'gracias para'.

quizQuestion:
Pagué diez euros {___} el libro.
(I paid ten euros for the book.)
sentenceTranslation: I paid ten euros for the book.
explanation: por — used for exchange or price. 'Pagué X por Y' = I paid X for Y. Always 'por' with pagué, vendí, cambié, etc. (never 'para').

quizQuestion:
Normalmente estudio {___} la tarde.
(I usually study in the afternoon.)
sentenceTranslation: I usually study in the afternoon.
grammarPointLabel: por — time of day (por la mañana/tarde/noche)
explanation: por — used with parts of the day: por la mañana (in the morning), por la tarde (in the afternoon/evening), por la noche (at night). Set expressions, always with 'por'.

quizQuestion:
Estaba enfermo, {___} no fui al trabajo.
(I was sick, that's why I didn't go to work.)
sentenceTranslation: I was sick, that's why I didn't go to work.
explanation: por eso — means 'because of that / that's why'. Common connector in Spanish. 'Por' expresses cause here.

quizQuestion:
Estudio {___} aprender español.
(I study in order to learn Spanish.)
sentenceTranslation: I study in order to learn Spanish.
explanation: para — used with infinitive to express purpose/goal. 'Estudio para aprender' = I study (in order) to learn. This is the most common use of 'para'.

quizQuestion:
Este regalo es {___} ti.
(This gift is for you.)
explanation: para — used for the recipient of something. 'Es para ti' = it's for you (you will receive it). Contrast: 'por ti' = for your sake (I acted on your behalf).

quizQuestion:
Mañana salgo {___} Madrid.
(Tomorrow I leave for Madrid.)
explanation: para — used for destination/direction when expressing travel toward a place. 'Salir para Madrid' = to leave for Madrid (destination). Different from 'ir a Madrid'.

quizQuestion:
{___} mí, es muy difícil.
(For me (in my opinion), it is very difficult.)
sentenceTranslation: For me (in my opinion), it is very difficult.
explanation: para — used for expressing one's perspective or opinion. 'Para mí' = in my opinion / from my perspective. Commonly used in conversation.

quizQuestion:
Este libro es {___} interesante.
(This book is very interesting.)
explanation: muy — used before adjectives and adverbs: muy interesante, muy bien. Never changes form. Contrast: mucho (used with nouns, changes gender/number).

quizQuestion:
Habla español {___} bien.
(He speaks Spanish very well.)
explanation: muy — used before adverbs too: muy bien (very well), muy rápido (very fast). Invariable.

quizQuestion:
Tengo {___} trabajo esta semana.
(I have a lot of work this week.)
sentenceTranslation: I have a lot of work this week.
explanation: mucho — used before masculine singular nouns: mucho trabajo, mucho tiempo. Agrees in gender/number with the noun.

quizQuestion:
Hay {___} gente en el mercado hoy.
(There are a lot of people at the market today.)
sentenceTranslation: There are a lot of people at the market today.
explanation: mucha — feminine form of mucho. Used before feminine singular nouns: mucha gente, mucha agua, mucha suerte. Must agree in gender.

quizQuestion:
Mi jefe trabaja {___}.
(My boss works a lot.)
explanation: mucho — when used as an adverb after a verb, mucho is invariable: trabaja mucho, come mucho, duerme mucho. Don't confuse with adjective use.

quizQuestion:
{___} mañana.
(I'm going to call you tomorrow.)
sentenceTranslation: I'm going to call you tomorrow.
grammarPointLabel: ir a + infinitivo (near future): voy a llamar
explanation: voy a llamar — near future structure. ir (present) + a + infinitive. Expresses planned or imminent future actions. More common than the future tense in spoken Spanish.

quizQuestion:
Mira esas nubes — {___}.
(Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.)
sentenceTranslation: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.
grammarPointLabel: ir a + infinitivo: va a llover (it's going to rain)
explanation: va a llover — third person of ir a + infinitivo. 'Va a llover' = it's going to rain. Weather expressions use the 3rd person singular impersonal.
